content.text: Data Retention During Service Decommissioning

When a Claude-integrated service is decommissioned, the data it has accumulated over its operational lifetime must be handled in accordance with applicable retention obligations before infrastructure is torn down. Deleting a service without a data retention review risks destroying records that are required by law (audit logs, financial transaction records, consent logs) and retaining data that should be deleted under privacy regulation (user PII, conversation transcripts). The data retention process for decommissioning identifies every data asset the service holds, maps each to its retention obligation, executes the required retention or deletion actions, and documents the outcome for compliance purposes.

attrs.alt: Data retention decision flow during decommissioning: identify asset, check legal hold, check statutory retention, then either preserve in legal archive, retain per schedule, or securely delete

content.text: Data Inventory: What a Claude-Integrated Service Holds

A Claude-integrated service accumulates several distinct categories of data during its operation. Each category has a different retention profile, different regulatory treatment, and different deletion complexity. The data inventory is the first step in the decommissioning process and must be completed before any deletion or archiving action is taken.
Data Category
What It Contains
Typical Retention Obligation
Deletion Complexity
Prompt logs
Raw API requests sent to Claude
None statutory; delete under privacy law if contains PII
Medium — may be in multiple log sinks
Conversation history
Full message threads per user session
Delete under GDPR/CCPA; retain if subject to legal hold
High — user-linked, needs per-user deletion
User PII
Names, emails, account identifiers
Delete unless financial/legal retention applies
High — may be embedded in logs and backups
Model outputs / generated content
Claude's responses stored by the service
Treat as user data if linked to user ID
Medium — often stored alongside prompt logs
Audit logs
Access events, admin actions, API calls
Retain 1–7 years depending on regulation
Low — archive, do not delete
Financial records
Billing, API usage invoices, cost allocation
7 years in most jurisdictions
Low — archive to accounting system
Consent and privacy records
User opt-ins, data processing agreements
Retain for duration of legal limitation period
Low — archive to legal repository
Data Classification and Retention Policy Mapping

Before executing any deletion or archiving action, each data asset must be classified and mapped to a retention policy. The classification determines the action: some assets must be archived, some must be deleted immediately, and some require conditional treatment depending on whether a legal hold applies. Implementing this as a code model makes the policy explicit, auditable, and repeatable across multiple decommissions.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class DataCategory(str, Enum):
    PROMPT_LOG           = "prompt_log"
    CONVERSATION_HISTORY = "conversation_history"
    USER_PII             = "user_pii"
    MODEL_OUTPUT         = "model_output"
    AUDIT_LOG            = "audit_log"
    FINANCIAL_RECORD     = "financial_record"
    CONSENT_RECORD       = "consent_record"

class RetentionAction(str, Enum):
    DELETE_IMMEDIATELY   = "delete_immediately"
    ARCHIVE              = "archive"
    ARCHIVE_THEN_DELETE  = "archive_then_delete"
    LEGAL_HOLD           = "legal_hold"

@dataclass
class RetentionPolicy:
    category: DataCategory
    default_action: RetentionAction
    retention_years: int | None        # None = delete immediately
    legal_hold_overrides: bool         # True = legal hold can override default action
    contains_pii: bool
    regulation_basis: str

RETENTION_POLICIES: dict[DataCategory, RetentionPolicy] = {
    DataCategory.PROMPT_LOG: RetentionPolicy(
        category=DataCategory.PROMPT_LOG,
        default_action=RetentionAction.DELETE_IMMEDIATELY,
        retention_years=None,
        legal_hold_overrides=True,
        contains_pii=True,
        regulation_basis="GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) / CCPA — minimise retention",
    ),
    DataCategory.CONVERSATION_HISTORY: RetentionPolicy(
        category=DataCategory.CONVERSATION_HISTORY,
        default_action=RetentionAction.DELETE_IMMEDIATELY,
        retention_years=None,
        legal_hold_overrides=True,
        contains_pii=True,
        regulation_basis="GDPR Art. 17 right to erasure",
    ),
    DataCategory.USER_PII: RetentionPolicy(
        category=DataCategory.USER_PII,
        default_action=RetentionAction.DELETE_IMMEDIATELY,
        retention_years=None,
        legal_hold_overrides=True,
        contains_pii=True,
        regulation_basis="GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) / CCPA",
    ),
    DataCategory.AUDIT_LOG: RetentionPolicy(
        category=DataCategory.AUDIT_LOG,
        default_action=RetentionAction.ARCHIVE,
        retention_years=7,
        legal_hold_overrides=False,
        contains_pii=False,
        regulation_basis="SOC 2 / ISO 27001 — 1-7 years depending on audit scope",
    ),
    DataCategory.FINANCIAL_RECORD: RetentionPolicy(
        category=DataCategory.FINANCIAL_RECORD,
        default_action=RetentionAction.ARCHIVE,
        retention_years=7,
        legal_hold_overrides=False,
        contains_pii=False,
        regulation_basis="IRS / HMRC / local tax authority — 7 years",
    ),
    DataCategory.CONSENT_RECORD: RetentionPolicy(
        category=DataCategory.CONSENT_RECORD,
        default_action=RetentionAction.ARCHIVE_THEN_DELETE,
        retention_years=5,
        legal_hold_overrides=True,
        contains_pii=True,
        regulation_basis="GDPR Art. 7 — demonstrable consent; statute of limitations",
    ),
}

def required_action(
    category: DataCategory,
    legal_hold_active: bool,
) -> RetentionAction:
    policy = RETENTION_POLICIES[category]
    if legal_hold_active and policy.legal_hold_overrides:
        return RetentionAction.LEGAL_HOLD
    return policy.default_action

The RETENTION_POLICIES dictionary is the authoritative source of truth for the decommissioning process. It is reviewed by the legal and privacy team before each decommission and updated whenever regulation changes. required_action applies the legal hold override: when a legal hold is active for the service being decommissioned, any data category where legal_hold_overrides is True must be preserved in the legal archive rather than deleted, even if the default policy is immediate deletion.
Secure Deletion Verification

Marking a database table as deleted does not constitute secure deletion. Soft deletes, database replication logs, object storage versioning, backup snapshots, and CDN caches can all retain copies of data that was nominally deleted. The secure deletion process must identify and address every storage location where the data exists, including secondary copies that were created automatically by the infrastructure.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class DeletionStorageType(str, Enum):
    PRIMARY_DATABASE     = "primary_database"
    READ_REPLICA         = "read_replica"
    OBJECT_STORAGE       = "object_storage"
    OBJECT_STORAGE_VERSIONS = "object_storage_versions"
    BACKUP_SNAPSHOTS     = "backup_snapshots"
    LOG_SINK             = "log_sink"          # e.g. CloudWatch, Datadog, Splunk
    CDN_CACHE            = "cdn_cache"
    VECTOR_STORE         = "vector_store"      # embeddings derived from prompt content

class DeletionStatus(str, Enum):
    PENDING   = "pending"
    COMPLETE  = "complete"
    CONFIRMED = "confirmed"   # independently verified after deletion
    FAILED    = "failed"

@dataclass
class StorageDeletionRecord:
    storage_type: DeletionStorageType
    storage_identifier: str      # e.g. "rds-prod-eu-west-1", "s3://bucket/prefix"
    records_deleted: int | None  # None if count not available (e.g. CDN purge)
    status: DeletionStatus
    verified_by: str | None = None
    notes: str = ""

@dataclass
class SecureDeletionPlan:
    data_category: str
    storage_locations: list[StorageDeletionRecord] = field(default_factory=list)

    def all_confirmed(self) -> bool:
        return all(r.status == DeletionStatus.CONFIRMED for r in self.storage_locations)

    def pending_locations(self) -> list[StorageDeletionRecord]:
        return [r for r in self.storage_locations
                if r.status in (DeletionStatus.PENDING, DeletionStatus.FAILED)]

    def deletion_summary(self) -> str:
        total = len(self.storage_locations)
        confirmed = sum(1 for r in self.storage_locations if r.status == DeletionStatus.CONFIRMED)
        return (
            f"{self.data_category}: {confirmed}/{total} locations confirmed. "
            + ("COMPLETE." if self.all_confirmed() else
               f"Pending: {[r.storage_identifier for r in self.pending_locations()]}")
        )

The vector_store storage type highlights a non-obvious secondary storage location: if the service stored embeddings derived from prompt or conversation content in a vector database for retrieval-augmented generation, those embeddings may implicitly encode the content of the original data. When the original data contains user PII and must be deleted under GDPR or CCPA, the embeddings are also within scope for deletion — not just the source text. The decommission plan must include the vector store as an explicit storage location even if it does not store raw text.
Decommission Compliance Record

The compliance record documents that the data retention process was executed correctly. It is the evidence that due process was followed — if a data subject submits a right-of-access or right-of-erasure request six months after a service was decommissioned, the compliance record proves that the data was deleted on a specific date by a specific process. Without this record, the organisation cannot demonstrate compliance even if deletion was executed correctly.

The is_complete method enforces three conditions simultaneously: legal hold was consulted (not just absent — the legal team must confirm no hold applies), the data inventory was completed before the service was decommissioned (not after, when evidence may already be gone), and all deletion plans report fully confirmed status across all storage locations. A compliance_status of COMPLIANT means all four conditions are met and the record is signed off by the legal or privacy officer. Store DecommissionRecord instances in a durable compliance repository — not in the same service being decommissioned, since that service's storage is being deleted.
Backup and Snapshot Handling

Automated backup systems create a specific challenge for data deletion during decommissioning. Database snapshots, S3 versioning, and log retention policies may hold copies of data at points in time when the data was legitimately retained — but if that data includes PII that must now be deleted, the backup copies are within scope. The three approaches differ in implementation complexity and regulatory risk.
Delete all backups:

The simplest approach. All snapshots and backup files are deleted along with the primary data. This satisfies deletion requirements for PII but loses the ability to restore from backup for any purpose — including legal hold compliance if a hold is asserted after decommissioning. Only appropriate when no legal hold is active or foreseeable.
Selective snapshot scrubbing:

The most defensible approach but also the most complex. A scrubbing process runs against each backup snapshot to remove PII fields while preserving non-PII data (audit trails, financial records). The scrubbed snapshots can be retained for legal purposes. Requires a per-backup-format scrubbing tool and generates a scrubbing certificate per snapshot.
Accept time-bounded retention of backup PII:

Some organisations accept that PII in automated backups will be deleted on the backup system's natural retention schedule (e.g., 30 or 90 days), rather than actively deleting it on the decommission date. This is legally risky in GDPR-scope jurisdictions where the right to erasure requires deletion of all copies, not just primary data. Document the legal basis if this approach is chosen.
Decommissioning Timeline and Team Coordination

Data retention tasks have dependencies on the order of decommissioning operations. The infrastructure teardown must not begin before the data retention process is complete — deleting the database before the compliance record is filed, or before all log sinks are purged, creates an irrecoverable gap. The decommission timeline must be structured so that data retention gates the infrastructure teardown, not the reverse.

The can_proceed_to_infra_teardown gate enforces the dependency: infrastructure teardown is blocked until all five data phases are complete. The gate_check method is the integration point for the deployment or change management system — a decommission change ticket that includes infrastructure deletion cannot be approved until this method returns CLEARED. Teams under time pressure to release infrastructure resources (to reduce costs) will sometimes attempt to skip data phases; the gate makes skipping visible and requires explicit override rather than allowing it by default.
Cross-Region and Multi-Environment Considerations

Production services deployed across multiple regions or environments hold data in each region independently. A decommission process that targets only the primary region leaves copies in secondary regions, disaster recovery replicas, and staging/pre-production environments untouched. The secure deletion plan must enumerate every environment and region where the service has data, not just the primary production deployment.
Environment Type
Common Oversight Risk
Mitigation
DR / failover region
Replicated database often forgotten in decommission scope
Enumerate all regions in data inventory step
Staging / pre-production
May hold production data subset used for testing
Confirm whether staging holds real PII; include if yes
Third-party integrations
Analytics, monitoring, or support tools that received data
Request deletion from each vendor per data processor agreements
Developer machines / CI artefacts
Test fixtures with real data, cached API responses
Audit for real data in test fixtures; delete or anonymise

Third-party integrations are the highest-risk oversight category. When a service sends user data to an external monitoring platform (for error context), a support tool (for ticket attachments), or an analytics pipeline (for behavioural data), those third parties are data processors under GDPR and have their own copies of the data. The decommission scope must include a request for data deletion from each processor, and the compliance record must include evidence of the deletion request and the processor's response. Processor deletion requests cannot be verified directly — the processor's written confirmation is the only available evidence.
Summary

Data retention during service decommissioning is a structured process that begins with a complete data inventory, maps each data category to its retention obligation, and executes either archiving or secure deletion for every storage location where the data exists. Claude-integrated services hold multiple data categories — prompt logs, conversation history, user PII, model outputs, audit logs, financial records, and consent records — each with distinct regulatory treatment. The decommission compliance record documents that the process was completed correctly before the service infrastructure was torn down, creating evidence that survives the service's deletion and can be produced in response to regulatory inquiries or data subject requests.
